content:
# Luke Chapter 7 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 93-95 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_007_page_001.jpg, LUK_007_page_002.jpg, LUK_007_page_003.jpg

---

**1** After he had completed all his words in the hearing of the people, he entered Capernaum.

**2** And a certain centurion had a servant who was dear to him, and he was ill and about to die.

**3** And having heard about Jesus, he sent to him elders of the Jews, asking him to come and save his servant.

**4** And when they came to Jesus, they entreated him earnestly, saying, "He is worthy that you should grant this to him,

**5** for he loves our nation, and he himself built the synagogue for us."

**6** And Jesus went with them. And when he was not far from the house, the centurion sent friends, saying to him, "Lord, do not trouble yourself, for I am not worthy that you should come under my roof.

**7** Therefore neither did I consider myself worthy to come to you. But say the word, and let my servant be healed.

**8** For I also am a man set under authority, having soldiers under me. I say to this one, 'Go,' and he goes; and to another, 'Come,' and he comes; and to my servant, 'Do this,' and he does it."

**9** And when Jesus heard these things, he marveled at him, and turning to the crowd following him, he said, "I say to you, not even in Israel have I found such great faith."

**10** And those who had been sent, having returned to the house, found the servant in good health.

**11** And it came to pass on the next day that he went to a city called Nain, and his disciples went with him, and a great crowd.

**12** And as he drew near to the gate of the city, behold, a dead man was being carried out, the only son of his mother, and she was a widow; and a considerable crowd from the city was with her.

**13** And when the Lord saw her, he had compassion on her and said to her, "Do not weep."

**14** And having come forward, he touched the bier, and the bearers stood still. And he said, "Young man, I say to you, arise."

**15** And the dead man sat up and began to speak. And he gave him to his mother.

**16** And awe seized them all, and they glorified God, saying, "A great prophet has risen among us," and, "God has visited his people."

**17** And this report concerning him went out through all Judea and through all the surrounding region.

**18** And the disciples of John reported to him concerning all these things.

**19** And John, having summoned two of his disciples, sent them to the Lord, saying, "Are you the Coming One, or are we to expect another?"

**20** And when the men came to him, they said, "John the Baptist sent us to you, saying, 'Are you the Coming One, or are we to expect another?'"

**21** In that hour he healed many of diseases and afflictions and evil spirits, and to many who were blind he granted sight.

**22** And he answered and said to them, "Go and report to John what you have seen and heard: the blind receive sight, the lame walk, lepers are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, the poor have good news proclaimed to them.

**23** And blessed is whoever is not offended on account of me."

**24** And when the messengers of John had departed, he began to speak to the crowds concerning John: "What did you go out into the wilderness to behold? A reed shaken by the wind?

**25** But what did you go out to see? A man clothed in soft garments? Behold, those who are in splendid clothing and luxury are in the palaces.

**26** But what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I say to you, and more than a prophet.

**27** This is he concerning whom it is written: 'Behold, I send my messenger before your face, who shall prepare your way before you.'

**28** I say to you, among those born of women, no one is greater than John. Yet the one who is least in the kingdom of God is greater than he."

**29** And all the people who heard, and the tax collectors, justified God, having been baptized with the baptism of John.

**30** But the Pharisees and the lawyers rejected the counsel of God for themselves, not having been baptized by him.

**31** "To what then shall I liken the people of this generation, and what are they like?

**32** They are like children sitting in the marketplace and calling to one another, saying, 'We played the flute for you, and you did not dance; we sang a dirge, and you did not weep.'

**33** For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, 'He has a demon.'

**34** The Son of Man has come eating and drinking, and you say, 'Behold, a man who is a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners!'

**35** Yet wisdom is justified by all her children."

**36** And one of the Pharisees asked him to eat with him. And having entered the house of the Pharisee, he reclined at table.

**37** And behold, a woman of the city who was a sinner, having learned that he was reclining at table in the house of the Pharisee, brought an alabaster flask of ointment.

**38** And standing behind him at his feet, weeping, she began to wet his feet with her tears, and she wiped them with the hair of her head, and she kissed his feet and anointed them with the ointment.

**39** And when the Pharisee who had invited him saw this, he said within himself, "If this man were a prophet, he would know who and what sort of woman this is who touches him -- that she is a sinner."

**40** And Jesus answered and said to him, "Simon, I have something to say to you." And he said, "Teacher, speak."

**41** "A certain moneylender had two debtors. The one owed five hundred denarii, and the other fifty.

**42** When they had nothing with which to repay, he graciously forgave them both. Which of them therefore will love him more?"

**43** Simon answered and said, "I suppose the one to whom he forgave more." And he said to him, "You have judged rightly."

**44** And turning to the woman, he said to Simon, "Do you see this woman? I entered your house; you gave me no water for my feet, but she has wet my feet with her tears and wiped them with her hair.

**45** You gave me no kiss, but she, from the time I entered, has not ceased kissing my feet.

**46** You did not anoint my head with oil, but she has anointed my feet with ointment.

**47** Therefore I say to you, her sins, which are many, have been forgiven -- for she loved much. But the one who is forgiven little, loves little."

**48** And he said to her, "Your sins have been forgiven."

**49** And those reclining at table with him began to say among themselves, "Who is this, who even forgives sins?"

**50** And he said to the woman, "Your faith has saved you. Go in peace."

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Consensus Methodology

SOPHIA evaluated all 50 verses across EMET (literal), LOGOS (formal), and PNEUMA (dynamic), with reference to the Codex Sinaiticus manuscript images (pages 93-95). The guiding principle remains consistent with Luke 6: fidelity to the Greek source as preserved in Sinaiticus, rendered in clear, dignified English that preserves both theological precision and natural readability.

### Manuscript Verification

The three Sinaiticus manuscript pages were examined. Page 1 (page 93) covers Luke 6:45 through approximately 7:16 across four columns. Page 2 (page 94) covers approximately 7:17 through 7:43. Page 3 (page 95) covers approximately 7:43 through 8:21. The text is written in Sinaiticus's characteristic four-column uncial format. All three agents read from the same manuscript pages, and no discrepancies in their readings of the base Greek text were detected. Disagreements were exclusively at the level of English rendering.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **7:1 -- "completed"**: All three agents agree on eplerosen as "completed" (EMET/LOGOS) or "finished" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA follows "completed" -- pleroo carries the sense of bringing to fullness, consistent with Luke 6 style.

2. **7:2 -- "servant" for doulos**: EMET and PNEUMA use "slave," LOGOS uses "servant." Luke himself alternates between doulos (vv. 2, 3, 8, 10) and pais (v. 7) for the same person. SOPHIA adopts "servant" for consistency throughout the passage, avoiding the anachronistic connotations of "slave" in modern English while accurately rendering the social role of a household dependent.

3. **7:2 -- "dear to him"**: EMET renders entimos as "valued by him," while LOGOS and PNEUMA render "dear to him." SOPHIA follows the two-agent consensus: "dear" captures both the affective and evaluative dimensions of entimos.

4. **7:3 -- "save"**: EMET renders diasose as "save-through," preserving the compound verb. LOGOS and PNEUMA render simply "save." SOPHIA follows the consensus: the intensified nuance of diasozo is adequately conveyed by context (the servant is near death) without requiring a hyphenated English compound.

5. **7:7 -- "say the word"**: EMET reads "say [it] with a word," LOGOS "speak a word," PNEUMA "just say the word." SOPHIA adopts "say the word" -- this preserves the instrumental dative (logo) while reading naturally. The centurion's point is that a single authoritative word suffices.

6. **7:9 -- "such great faith"**: EMET reads "such great faith" (tosautem pistin), LOGOS "such faith," PNEUMA "faith like this." Sinaiticus reads tosautem pistin, where tosoutos means "so great, so much." SOPHIA follows EMET: the adjective explicitly conveys magnitude, and rendering it as "such great faith" is faithful to the Greek without redundancy, since "such" alone in English does not necessarily convey greatness.

7. **7:11 -- "on the next day"**: EMET and LOGOS specify "on the next day" (en te hexes). PNEUMA loosens to "soon afterward." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: the Greek provides a precise temporal link that should be preserved.

8. **7:12 -- "only son"**: EMET reads "only-begotten" (monogenes), LOGOS and PNEUMA read "only son." SOPHIA follows the majority: monogenes in this context means "one and only child," distinct from the theological "only-begotten" associated with Johannine usage.

9. **7:13 -- "had compassion on her"**: EMET and LOGOS use "had compassion." PNEUMA renders "his heart broke for her" (esplagchnisthe). SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: while PNEUMA evocatively captures the visceral force of splagchnizomai, "had compassion" maintains the formal register established in Luke 6 and avoids interpretive expansion.

10. **7:14 -- "arise"**: Three-way disagreement. EMET reads "be raised" (egertheti, aorist passive imperative), LOGOS "arise," PNEUMA "get up." SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "arise" preserves the solemnity of a command addressed to a dead man, while remaining immediately intelligible. EMET's passive is theologically precise but awkward in English direct address; PNEUMA's "get up" is too colloquial for a resurrection command.

11. **7:16 -- "awe" over "fear"**: EMET and LOGOS render phobos as "fear." PNEUMA renders "awe." SOPHIA overrides the two-agent majority: the immediate context -- glorifying God and declaring a prophet has risen -- indicates reverent wonder leading to praise, not terror. Phobos encompasses both senses; context determines which predominates.

12. **7:16 -- "has risen" over "has been raised"**: EMET reads "has been raised" and LOGOS "has been raised up" (both preserving the passive egerthe). PNEUMA reads "has risen." SOPHIA overrides the majority: the crowd's spontaneous exclamation is better served by the active "has risen." The theological passive is present in the Greek, but the crowd is expressing awe at the appearance of a prophet, not articulating a theology of divine agency.

13. **7:16 -- "visited"**: EMET and LOGOS render epeskepsato as "visited." PNEUMA renders "come to the aid of." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: "visited" preserves the theologically loaded term that echoes Luke 1:68 and 1:78, maintaining the Lukan motif of divine visitation.

14. **7:19 -- "expect"**: EMET reads "are-we-to-expect" (prosdokomen), LOGOS "look for," PNEUMA "expect." SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA: "expect" is precise for prosdokao, which means to anticipate or await.

15. **7:22 -- "lepers"**: EMET and LOGOS retain "lepers" (leproi). PNEUMA expands to "those with skin diseases." SOPHIA follows the majority: "lepers" is the direct rendering and preserves the Isaianic allusion (Isa 35:5-6; 61:1).

16. **7:25 -- "in the palaces"**: EMET reads "in the palaces," LOGOS "in the palaces of kings," PNEUMA "in royal palaces." Sinaiticus reads en tois basileiois -- the substantive adjective basileios itself means "royal/kingly." SOPHIA follows EMET's simpler rendering: "palaces" in English already carries regal connotation, and basileiois does not contain a separate word for "kings."

17. **7:29 -- "justified God"**: EMET and LOGOS preserve "justified God" (edikaiosan ton theon). PNEUMA paraphrases as "affirmed God's justice." SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS: the striking Greek expression, where humans "justify" God, should be preserved rather than explained.

18. **7:31 -- "liken"**: EMET and LOGOS read "shall I liken" (homoioso). PNEUMA reads "what comparison fits." SOPHIA retains "liken" -- while less common in modern speech, it is not archaic in biblical register and preserves the direct sense of homoioo more precisely than alternatives.

19. **7:32 -- "played the flute"**: EMET reads "piped" (eulesamen). LOGOS and PNEUMA read "played the flute." SOPHIA follows the majority: "played the flute" clarifies the imagery for modern readers.

20. **7:34 -- "drunkard"**: EMET reads "wine-drinker" (oinopotes). LOGOS and PNEUMA read "drunkard." SOPHIA follows the majority: the opponents' accusation carries pejorative force echoing Deuteronomy 21:20, and "drunkard" captures that accusatory tone.

21. **7:35 -- "justified" and tense**: EMET renders "was justified" (aorist), LOGOS "is justified" (gnomic present), PNEUMA "is vindicated." SOPHIA adopts LOGOS's gnomic "is justified" -- the saying has proverbial quality, and "justified" maintains the verbal echo with edikaiosan in v. 29.

22. **7:37 -- "ointment"**: EMET and LOGOS render myron as "ointment." PNEUMA renders "perfumed oil." SOPHIA follows the majority: "ointment" is the established biblical rendering and maintains consistency through the passage (vv. 37, 38, 46).

23. **7:39 -- "said within himself"**: EMET reads "said within himself," LOGOS and PNEUMA "said to himself." SOPHIA follows EMET: Sinaiticus reads eipen en heauto, and "within himself" more precisely reflects the prepositional phrase en heauto, emphasizing that the thought remained internal.

24. **7:42 -- "graciously forgave"**: EMET and PNEUMA render echarisato as "graciously forgave." LOGOS renders simply "forgave." SOPHIA follows EMET/PNEUMA: the verb charizomai explicitly conveys grace (charis), and preserving this dimension links the forgiveness of debts to the granting of sight in v. 21 (same verb).

25. **7:47 -- "for she loved much"**: The most significant translation decision in the chapter. EMET renders "because she loved much," LOGOS "for she loved much," PNEUMA "her great love proves it." The Greek hoti egapesen polu is ambiguous: does love cause forgiveness or evidence it? The parable (vv. 41-43) establishes that forgiveness precedes and produces love. SOPHIA follows LOGOS: "for she loved much" preserves the Greek syntax while the parabolic context clarifies the logic. PNEUMA's rendering is theologically correct but goes beyond translation into commentary.

26. **7:48 -- "have been forgiven"**: EMET and LOGOS render the perfect passive apheontai as "have been forgiven." PNEUMA renders "are forgiven." SOPHIA follows the majority: the perfect tense is theologically significant -- the forgiveness is a completed state being publicly declared, not a new act.

## Disagreement Log

| Verse | Issue | EMET (Literal) | LOGOS (Formal) | PNEUMA (Dynamic) | SOPHIA Decision | Rationale |
|-------|-------|----------------|----------------|-------------------|-----------------|-----------|
| 7:2 | entimos rendering | "valued by him" | "dear to him" | "dear to him" | "dear to him" | 2-to-1; captures both affective and evaluative senses |
| 7:2 | doulos rendering | "slave" | "servant" | "slave" | "servant" | Consistency; avoids anachronistic connotations |
| 7:3 | diasose | "save-through" | "save" | "save" | "save" | 2-to-1; compound nuance implicit in context |
| 7:7 | eipe logo | "say [it] with a word" | "speak a word" | "just say the word" | "say the word" | Preserves instrumental dative; natural English |
| 7:9 | tosautem pistin | "such great faith" | "such faith" | "faith like this" | "such great faith" | tosoutos = "so great"; EMET most faithful |
| 7:11 | en te hexes | "on the next [day]" | "on the next day" | "soon afterward" | "on the next day" | Preserves temporal precision; PNEUMA too vague |
| 7:12 | monogenes | "only-begotten" | "only son" | "only son" | "only son" | 2-to-1; contextual meaning, not Johannine theological use |
| 7:13 | esplagchnisthe | "had compassion" | "had compassion" | "his heart broke" | "had compassion" | 2-to-1; avoids interpretive expansion |
| 7:14 | egertheti | "be raised" | "arise" | "get up" | "arise" | 3-way split; balances dignity, clarity, theology |
| 7:16 | phobos | "fear" | "fear" | "awe" | "awe" | SOPHIA override; context = reverent wonder, not terror |
| 7:16 | egerthe (crowd) | "has been raised" | "has been raised up" | "has risen" | "has risen" | SOPHIA override; crowd exclamation, not theological formula |
| 7:16 | epeskepsato | "visited" | "visited" | "come to the aid of" | "visited" | 2-to-1; preserves Lukan visitation motif |
| 7:17 | logos | "this word" | "this report" | "this report" | "this report" | 2-to-1; logos as news = "report" |
| 7:19 | prosdokomen | "expect" | "look for" | "expect" | "expect" | 2-to-1; more precise for prosdokao |
| 7:21 | echarisato blepein | "granted to see" | "granted sight" | "granted sight" | "granted sight" | 2-to-1; natural English preserving charizomai |
| 7:22 | leproi | "lepers" | "lepers" | "those with skin diseases" | "lepers" | 2-to-1; direct rendering; Isaianic echo |
| 7:23 | skandalisthe en emoi | "offended in me" | "offended on account of me" | "stumble over me" | "offended on account of me" | LOGOS most precise; "in me" awkward; "stumble" shifts metaphor |
| 7:25 | en tois basileiois | "in the palaces" | "in the palaces of kings" | "in royal palaces" | "in the palaces" | basileiois = royal palaces; "palaces" suffices |
| 7:29 | edikaiosan ton theon | "justified God" | "justified God" | "affirmed God's justice" | "justified God" | 2-to-1; preserves striking Greek expression |
| 7:30 | nomikoi | "lawyers" | "lawyers" | "experts in the law" | "lawyers" | 2-to-1; concise; Luke's own lexical distinction |
| 7:31 | homoioso | "shall I liken" | "shall I liken" | "what comparison fits" | "shall I liken" | 2-to-1; "liken" is clear in biblical register |
| 7:32 | eulesamen | "piped" | "played the flute" | "played the flute" | "played the flute" | 2-to-1; clearer for modern readers |
| 7:34 | oinopotes | "wine-drinker" | "drunkard" | "drunkard" | "drunkard" | 2-to-1; pejorative accusatory force; Deut 21:20 echo |
| 7:35 | edikaiothe tense | "was justified" | "is justified" | "is vindicated" | "is justified" | Gnomic present; proverbial quality; "justified" echoes v.29 |
| 7:37 | myron | "ointment" | "ointment" | "perfumed oil" | "ointment" | 2-to-1; established biblical rendering |
| 7:39 | eipen en heauto | "said within himself" | "said to himself" | "said to himself" | "said within himself" | EMET most precise for en heauto |
| 7:41 | denaria | "denarii" | "denarii" | "silver coins" | "denarii" | 2-to-1; Greek loanword; universally understood |
| 7:42 | echarisato | "graciously forgave" | "forgave" | "graciously forgave" | "graciously forgave" | 2-to-1; preserves charis root; links to v.21 |
| 7:47 | hoti egapesen polu | "because she loved much" | "for she loved much" | "her great love proves it" | "for she loved much" | Preserves Greek syntax; parable clarifies logic |
| 7:48 | apheontai tense | "have been forgiven" | "have been forgiven" | "are forgiven" | "have been forgiven" | 2-to-1; perfect tense = completed enduring state |
| 7:49 | en heautois | "within themselves" | "among themselves" | "among themselves" | "among themselves" | 2-to-1; communal setting favors "among" |

### Resolution Statistics

- **Total disagreements identified:** 31
- **Resolved by two-agent agreement:** 22 (71%)
- **Resolved against two-agent majority (SOPHIA override):** 3 (10%) -- vv. 16 phobos, 16 egerthe, 39 en heauto
- **Resolved among three-way disagreement:** 6 (19%) -- vv. 7, 9, 14, 23, 25, 47
- **EMET adopted:** 5 times (vv. 9, 16 egerthe, 25, 31, 39)
- **LOGOS adopted:** 14 times
- **PNEUMA adopted:** 2 times (vv. 16 phobos, 19)
- **Blended/independent formulation:** 10 times

---
*Compiled by SOPHIA in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA, from Codex Sinaiticus (4th century CE).*
